T-6G Texan · Daytona, FL · 30+ Years of McNeely Racing

One of American air racing's most storied programs. Built across three decades by a family that lives and breathes the T-6. Greg McNeely carries Race #90 into the new era at Roswell — competing at 219+ mph in front of 120,000 fans.

Race #90

Greg McNeely — The Pilot

Greg McNeely

FedEx Captain · Air Racer · Race #90 · Daytona, FL

Greg McNeely grew up in the cockpit alongside his father Gene. The son of a 26,000-hour aviator and 24-year AeroShell slot pilot, Greg built his own career on top of that foundation — not alongside it.

As a FedEx Captain, Greg brings thousands of hours of precision professional flying to the race line. The discipline required at the FedEx flight deck — instrument procedures, system management under pressure, sustained decision-making — is exactly what separates the front of the T-6 pack from the rest. Racing is not his job. It's what he does with the skills his job demands.

His record: 1st in the 2019 Reno Silver race, Race #90 across the line ahead of the field. Silver at the final Reno NCAR in 2023 at 219.280 mph in the T-6 Gold race. Then the inaugural Roswell NCAR 2025, carrying the McNeely program into the new era. Now 2026 — all seven classes, the Thunderbirds, and Race #90 on the course.

In 2026 Greg supports the Titan Aerobatic Team as ride aircraft at Sun 'n Fun — a direct continuation of the McNeely family's 30-year T-6 airshow legacy.

1st
2019 Reno Silver Race Winner
219 mph
2023 T-6 Gold Silver — final Reno NCAR
FedEx
Captain — professional aviator
2025 + 2026
Roswell NCAR competitor
Gene McNeely — 1942–2025

Where It All Started

Gene McNeely fell in love with flying as a kid at the Ocala Airport. After serving in the US Navy he finished his certificates on the G.I. Bill and never stopped. 26,000+ flight hours followed across agricultural aviation, air cargo in DC-3s and Beech 18s and MU-2s, homebuilt aircraft construction, air racing, and 24 years in the slot position of the AeroShell Aerobatic Team.

He joined the North American Aerobatic Team in 1994 replacing the retiring Ben Cunningham. As the team grew to four aircraft he took the slot. In 2001 he secured Shell Aviation as title sponsor — creating the AeroShell Aerobatic Team. Gene understood the business: "Without a sponsorship, you're just flying as a hobby." He built the model Greg now carries forward.

He raced T-6s at Reno for over 30 years. He passed away July 5, 2025, age 83, at his home in Port Orange, Florida. The Titan Aerobatic Team posted: "Gene always had a smile, a project and a scheme. He was one of a kind. We're heartbroken, but honoured to have shared the sky with him."

Career Highlights
US Navy veteran · G.I. Bill pilot certificates
Agricultural aviation — 20+ years
Air cargo — DC-3, Beech 18, MU-2
North American Aerobatic Team — 1994
AeroShell slot pilot — 1994–2017
AeroShell sponsorship architect — 2001
Reno NCAR T-6 class — 30+ years
T-6 80th Anniversary Solo Demo — 2018 STIHL NCAR
Homebuilt aircraft builder
26,000+ total flight hours
Gene McNeely — AT-6 Racing Texan (2011)Gene presents Race #90's predecessor — race-tuned Pratt & Whitney R-1340. Turn it up.
The World's Fastest Motorsport

Air Racing: America's Original Sport

Before NASCAR. Before Indianapolis. Before Le Mans in its modern form — there was air racing. 1909 in France. Cleveland in 1929. Reno in 1964. Roswell in 2025. One unbroken line of American motorsport that predates everything else by decades.

1909
The First Air Race
Grande Semaine d'Aviation de la Champagne, Reims, France. The Wright Brothers' aircraft are four years old. Glenn Curtiss wins the Gordon Bennett Trophy at 47 mph. A sport is born that predates every American motorsport by decades.
Smithsonian Air & Space Museum →
1929
Cleveland & the Thompson Trophy
National Air Races arrive in Cleveland. The Thompson Trophy closed-course pylon race debuts. 100,000 spectators per day. Jimmy Doolittle, Roscoe Turner, the Gee Bee racers. Aviation's golden age at full speed.
Cleveland Air Show — Racing History →
1932
Doolittle at 253 mph
Jimmy Doolittle wins the Thompson Trophy in a Gee Bee R-1 at 253 mph — faster than most military fighters of the era. The Smithsonian holds the original trophy.
Smithsonian Pioneers of Flight →
1941
The T-6 Texan
North American Aviation's T-6 Texan enters mass production. Over 15,000 built. Every Allied pilot who flew in WWII trained in one. The aircraft Greg McNeely races today is a direct descendant.
Smithsonian — T-6G Texan Collection →
1948
NASCAR Founded
NASCAR holds its first season. Air racing has been a professional competitive sport for nearly 40 years. Perspective matters when someone tells you air racing is a niche sport.
1964
Reno: 59 Seasons Begin
The National Championship Air Races launch at Reno-Stead Airport, Nevada. A 59-year run begins. Seven classes. P-51 Mustangs at 500+ mph. The T-6 class grows into the crowd favorite — "the Pilot's Class."
airrace.org — Official NCAR →
1994
Gene McNeely Joins the Team
Gene joins the North American Aerobatic Team, flying right wing then slot. In 2001 he secures Shell Aviation as title sponsor — the team becomes the AeroShell Aerobatic Team, one of the longest-running civilian aerobatic teams in history.
Titan Aerobatic Team →
2019
Greg Wins the Silver Race
Greg McNeely, Race #90 Undecided, wins the Reno T-6 Silver race. Ahead of the field. A clear statement of where Race #90 stands in the competitive T-6 class.
T-6 Racing Association — Race Results →
2023
Final Reno — Greg Takes Silver in Gold
The 59th and final NCAR at Reno-Stead. Greg finishes Silver in the T-6 Gold race at 219.280 mph. The Reno era ends after 59 years. Gene McNeely attends his last Reno. Greg carries the flag.
NCAR Official Results →
2025–26
The Roswell Era
NCAR moves to Roswell Air Center, NM. Greg races the inaugural 2025 event. In 2026 all seven classes return with USAF Thunderbirds headlining. September 16–20, 2026 — Race #90 on the course.
airrace.org — 2026 NCAR →
2018 Reno T-6 Silver RaceRace #90 Undecided and #89 Baby Boomer — father and son together on the Reno course. Miss TnT, Big Red, Gotcha, Abracadabra.
AeroShell Night Show — EAA Oshkosh 2016Gene McNeely in the slot. Cameras mounted on aircraft. The show he called "absolutely incredible to look down and see all the cameras flashing."
Reno Air Races 2023 — The Final YearThe last races at Reno-Stead. T-6 class, Unlimited, jets. Greg's Silver finish weekend.
AeroShell / Titan Team Full Performance — Oshkosh 2023The Titan Aerobatic Team's performance — the team Gene helped build and that Greg supports in 2026.

The Aircraft

Race #90 — T-6G Texan

The North American T-6G Texan. Built to train Allied pilots in WWII. Raced at the NCAR for 30+ years by the McNeely family. White with checkered cowl. Now carrying the program into Roswell.
About the T-6 Class

The Pilot's Class

The T-6 class is known in the pit as the Pilot's Class. Every aircraft is a variant of the North American Texan — AT-6, SNJ, Harvard, T-6G. Tight rules: no superchargers, limited engine modifications. The difference between a podium finish and mid-pack is the pilot.

Race speeds range from 200–250 mph. The Roswell course is roughly 5.3 miles in circumference. Pilots race 50 feet off the ground around 9-foot pylons. Eight aircraft at a time, heat races building to Gold, Silver, and Bronze finals over five days.

The T-6 class has historically been the most popular with grandstand spectators — loud, fast, visually dramatic, and slow enough to track with the naked eye. Five laps, 10–15 minutes of sustained racing. When Race #90 comes off the last pylon and heads for the start/finish, that's your logo at 219+ mph.

Race #90 — Aircraft Data
Type: North American T-6G Texan
Race number: #90
Race name: Undecided
Home base: Daytona, Florida
Engine: Pratt & Whitney R-1340 Wasp (600 hp)
Class: NCAR T-6 / Pilot's Class
Best race speed: 219.280 mph (2023 Gold Silver)
Pilot: Greg McNeely

Current Sponsors — Race #90

Every brand whose logo currently appears on Race #90. All 2026 positions are open — no existing arrangement carries forward without a confirmed cash commitment. First confirmed wins.

Title Position — Mid Fuselage
Honda Generators
World's most trusted portable generator brand. Honda reliability and engineering — a natural partner for an aircraft that demands the same.
powerequipment.honda.com →
Forward Fuselage
Concorde Battery
Leading manufacturer of AGM aircraft batteries. Used in military and civil aviation worldwide. Powering Race #90's electrical system.
concordebattery.com →
Cockpit Panel
Bose Aviation
#1 pilot-preferred aviation headset. Active noise cancellation for the cockpit of every aircraft Greg flies — from Race #90 to the FedEx flight deck.
bose.com/aviation →
Turtle Deck
Lincoln Welders
Lincoln Electric — world leader in welding and cutting. A natural partner for aircraft construction and the mechanical heritage of T-6 racing.
lincolnelectric.com →
Lower Cowl
Goodyear Aviation
Goodyear aircraft tires — trusted on everything from light GA to commercial aviation. On Race #90 for every taxi, landing, and pit display.
goodyear.com/aviation →
Lower Cowl
ICOM Aviation
ICOM aviation radios — the communication system of choice for pilots worldwide. Race communication is life-critical; ICOM is on the aircraft.
icomamerica.com/aviation →
Mid Fuselage
Kimmel Aviation Insurance
KAI — founded 1972 insuring crop-dusters. Now one of the largest aviation insurance agencies in the world. Licensed in all 50 states.
kimmelsaviation.com →

Mid-Continent Instruments
Certified avionics and instrument manufacturing, repair, and overhaul. Flight-critical systems for general aviation nationwide.
midcontinent.com →

Engine overhaul, sales, and parts for radial engines — including the Pratt & Whitney R-1340 Wasp that powers Race #90. A natural partnership.

STC'd alternator replacements for certified and experimental aircraft. Reliable electrical power at the voltages racing demands.

aviationcovers.com →

Aircraft seatbelts and harness systems for aerobatic and racing aircraft. When racing at 219 mph 50 feet off the ground, your harness matters.
